3 min read
Just now

--

Polyrepo (Multiple Repositories) is an architectural pattern where every microservice, frontend client, or infrastructure component resides in its own dedicated and independent Git repository.
Structure Example:

• auth-service Repository 1

• billing-service Repository 2

• web-frontend Repository 3
Polyrepo Implementation Plan
In a polyrepo system, managing CI/CD and deployment is divided into two core phases: Pipeline Code Management (how pipelines are written) and Deployment Execution (how code reaches the server).
Part 1: Pipeline Code Management (How to Write & Manage Pipelines)
Managing CI/CD configurations (.gitlab-ci.yml or GitHub Actions workflows) across multiple repositories generally follows one of three patterns:
1. The “Copy-Paste” Anti-Pattern (Decentralized)

• Process: Manually copying and pasting a 100-line CI/CD YAML configuration into every service repository (auth-service, billing-service, frontend, etc.).

• Reality Check: Easy at first, but when managing 15 microservices, a minor update to a Docker build step or base image requires opening 15 separate PRs. It quickly becomes a severe maintenance bottleneck.
2. Centralized CI Repo / Templates (The Enterprise Standard)

• Process: Creating a dedicated repository (e.g., ci-templates) containing core, reusable pipeline configurations (Docker builds, security scans, deployment scripts). Service repos simply "include" or "call" these templates while passing their own dynamic environment variables.

• Reality Check: The production-grade standard. It converts CI/CD into a versioned platform product that can be centrally maintained and updated.
3. Cloud-Native Buildpacks / Auto-DevOps

• Process: Omitting Dockerfiles and complex CI/CD YAML files entirely. The build tool (like Paketo Buildpacks or Auto DevOps) automatically detects the programming language upon code push and compiles the container image.

• Reality Check: Fast to set up, but loses granular control over the build process. Not ideal for complex backend architectures requiring custom JVM tuning or native dependencies.
Part 2: Deployment Execution (How Code Reaches the Server)
In a production Polyrepo environment, delivering compiled container images to the server follows one of three main strategies:
1. Push-Based Deployment (SSH Method)

• Process: The CI Runner builds the Docker image, pushes it to a registry, and directly SSHs into the production server to execute commands (e.g., docker compose up -d).

• The Cost: High security risk. Requires storing production SSH credentials inside the CI tool. If the CI tool is compromised, attackers gain root access to the server. Having multiple repos open SSH connections to production servers creates an unnecessary attack vector.
2. Pull-Based / GitOps (The Industry Standard)

• Process: The CI pipeline’s job ends after building and pushing the image to a registry (Docker Hub / ECR). The pipeline never directly touches the production server. A lightweight agent on the server (like Watchtower, ArgoCD, or Flux) polls the registry and automatically pulls new image tags to restart containers.

• The Advantage: Production server keys are never exposed to the CI tool. The server independently controls its own updates.
3. Meta-repo / Integration Manifest (Enterprise Pattern)

• Process: Individual microservices only build and tag their images. They commit their updated image tag to a central integration-repo containing a release.yaml manifest file. The deployment orchestrator reads this central manifest to validate and deploy the entire system state together.

• The Advantage: Provides system-level predictability and atomic releases. Guarantees exactly which service versions are tested and deployed together. Higher complexity, but maximum system stability.
